Inference: Extend default cuda-graph coverage to 512 tokens (#5797)

findings.claim: This commit adds a new `cuda_graph_max_tokens` knob (default 512) and changes the default prefill/mixed CUDA-graph token bound from `max_requests*(num_speculative_tokens+1)` (decode bound) to `min(max(512, decode_bound), max_tokens)`. When CUDA graphs are enabled AND the decode bound is < 512, this WIDENS the captured prefill/mixed graph range at startup, increasing the number and size of graphs captured (up to 512 tokens instead of the decode bound). This raises one-time capture time and CUDA-graph mempool memory. However: (1) it is NOT on the per-step hot path — capture is a one-time initialization cost; (2) the default `num_cuda_graphs=None` means graphs are only generated when the caller opts in / sets num_cuda_graphs, so it is not universally on the default path; (3) the wider prefill coverage is generally a throughput WIN at runtime (fewer eager fallbacks for mid-size prefill/mixed batches). The only downside is extra startup capture time and CUDA-graph pool memory (bounded by 512 tokens and clamped to max_tokens), which could matter on tight-GPU-memory small-batch configs. Not a runtime regression on the steady-state decode/replay path.

Missing moe_router_dtype causes unexpected downcast in ModelOpt example (#5810)
IMA fix by making the copy of book keeping buffer to GPU blocking (#5715)

findings.claim: The commit changes the single coalesced H2D copy of the ~71KB pinned bookkeeping buffer in transfer_bookkeeping_to_gpu() from non_blocking=True to non_blocking=False, which introduces a per-step host<->device blocking sync on the inference decode hot path (transfer_bookkeeping_to_gpu is called every step, after initialize_attention_state, before every forward). This is technically a per-step defensive-oversync pattern. However, it is a legitimate correctness fix for an illegal-memory-access (IMA) race: the pinned buffer is re-staged in place on the next step, so an async copy can be clobbered by host writes. The PR states the buffer is small (~71KB, ~3-5us on PCIe Gen4) and benchmarks show no measurable generation-throughput difference vs an async double-buffered copy. Given the fix is default-enabled on the hot path but the added cost is negligible and it fixes a hard correctness bug (hang/crash), this is at most a minor suggestion, not a real regression.

Inference: Do not route pad/dummy tokens to any expert (#4922)
Inference: Add load aware routing to prefix caching.  (#5607)

findings.claim: This commit changes the default `prefix_caching_coordinator_policy` from `FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK` to `LOAD_BALANCED`, so DP-inference jobs that never set the flag inherit a different routing policy. However this is NOT a runtime perf regression per the three-question test: (1) Hot path: routing runs once per request in `get_best_data_parallel_rank`, not per-token/per-step. (2) Caller: invoked from `start()` on SUBMIT_REQUEST — one call per client request. (3) Default: yes it is now the default, but LOAD_BALANCED simply routes to the least-loaded rank (`free_slots / max_requests` via `_pending_counts`) and does not run heavier work than the prior default. The scoring math (`_match_vector`, `lexsort`) is the same code path used before for non-round-robin policies; LOAD_BALANCED actually skips prefix-affinity matching. The new default is arguably cheaper per request, so no regression is evident. The change is a routing-quality/behavioral default change, not a compute-cost regression on a hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.
